title: Routes of Drug Administration
topics: طرق الإعطاء الوريدية والعضلية والتحت جلدية
الطرق الأخرى: داخل المفصل، داخل القراب الشوكي
summary: تُقسَّم طرق إعطاء الأدوية إلى Enteral (عبر الجهاز الهضمي: فموي، تحت اللسان، شدقي، شرجي) و Parenteral (خارج الجهاز الهضمي: وريدي، عضلي، تحت جلدي، جلدي، وغيرها). الطريق الوريدي (IV) هو الوحيد الذي يعطي توافراً حيوياً 100% لأنه يُدخل الدواء مباشرة في الدوران. أهم ميزة للطريق تحت اللسان تجنب التأثير الأول للكبد (First-pass metabolism) وسرعة امتصاصه بسبب تدفق الدم الغني. الطريق الفموي هو الأكثر أماناً واقتصاداً لكنه يخضع للتأثير الأول ويتأثر بالإنزيمات الهضمية وحمض المعدة.

key_drugs.name: Nitroglycerin
key_drugs.note: مثال على Transdermal patch - إطلاق مستدام جلدياً لعلاج الذبحة الصدرية

key_drugs.name: Nicotine patch
key_drugs.note: لصقة جلدية للمساعدة على الإقلاع عن التدخين

key_drugs.name: Propranolol
key_drugs.note: الجرعة الفموية أكبر 20-40 مرة من الجرعة الوريدية بسبب First-pass metabolism

key_drugs.name: Beclomethasone
key_drugs.note: تُعطى بالاستنشاق لتأثير موضعي في الرئتين

key_drugs.name: Corticosteroids
key_drugs.note: تُحقن داخل المفصل (Intra-articular) في التهاب المفاصل الروماتويدي

mnemonics.title: طرق الحقن الأربعة الرئيسية - IIIS
mnemonics.body:
IV = Intravenous = وريدي (100% bioavailability)
IM = Intramuscular = عضلي (< 100%)
SC = Subcutaneous = تحت جلدي (< 100%، أبطأ)
ID = Intradermal = داخل الأدمة (للاختبارات)
تذكر بالترتيب من الأسرع للأبطأ: IV > IM > SC > ID

mnemonics.title: الطريق الفموي والـ First-pass
mnemonics.body: تخيل الدواء الفموي كشخص يريد الوصول للمدينة (الدوران الجهازي). لكن عليه أولاً المرور بحاجز الكبد (First-pass metabolism). Propranolol مثلاً يحتاج 20-40 ضعفاً من الجرعة الفموية مقارنة بالوريدية لأن الكبد يهضمه كثيراً. أما تحت اللسان فالطريق مباشر: الدم تحت اللسان يصب مباشرة في الجهازي متجاوزاً الكبد.

mnemonics.title: مزايا IV - REBSPA
mnemonics.body:
R = Rapid action (فعل سريع)
E = Emergency use (للطوارئ)
B = Bypass liver (يتجاوز الكبد)
S = Suitable for large volumes (مناسب للأحجام الكبيرة)
P = Proper dose adjustment (ضبط دقيق للجرعة)
A = Available for unconscious patients (للمريض غير المتعاون)
عيبه: مرتفع الخطورة، يحتاج معقماً، صعب العكس

mnemonics.title: مقارنة الطرق بالسلم - السرعة
mnemonics.body: تخيل سلم السرعة: في الأعلى IV (مباشر في الوريد = أسرع)، ثم Inhalation، ثم Sublingual، ثم IM، ثم SC، وفي القاع Oral و Transdermal (الأبطأ). كلما كان الطريق أقرب للدوران الجهازي مباشرة كلما كان أسرع.

mnemonics.title: Enteral vs Parenteral
mnemonics.body:
Enteral = تمر بالأمعاء (Entero = الأمعاء): فموي، تحت اللسان، شدقي، شرجي
Parenteral = تتجاوز الأمعاء (Para = بجانب/خارج): وريدي، عضلي، تحت جلدي، جلدي، استنشاق
تذكر: Para = تجاوز = حقن بالإبرة وما شابهها

flashcards.front: Which is the only route that gives Bioavailability = 100%?
flashcards.back: The intravenous (IV) route, because it delivers the drug directly into the systemic circulation.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between IV Bolus and IV Infusion?
flashcards.back:
Bolus: the full dose given rapidly = high plasma concentration and rapid effect.
Infusion: a continuous constant rate = unlimited volume.

flashcards.front: Why is IM injection contraindicated in patients on anticoagulants?
flashcards.back: Intramuscular (IM) injection is the one contraindicated in these patients (risk of hematoma), not IV.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum volume for subcutaneous (SC) injection?
flashcards.back: Approximately 1 ml.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum volume for intramuscular (IM) injection?
flashcards.back: Approximately 2–5 ml.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of the SC route over IV?
flashcards.back: Longer duration of action due to slower release into the circulation; suitable for suspensions and oily solutions.

flashcards.front: What is Intradermal administration? Why is it rarely used for systemic effect?
flashcards.back: Injection into the dermis, used mainly for diagnostic tests (e.g., Mantoux test for TB). Rarely used systemically due to poor distribution into the bloodstream.

flashcards.front: What is First-pass metabolism? How does it affect the oral dose of Propranolol?
flashcards.back: Breakdown of a drug in the liver before it reaches the systemic circulation. Propranolol requires an oral dose 20–40 times higher than the IV dose.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of the sublingual route over the oral route?
flashcards.back: Faster absorption due to rich vascularity; drains directly into the systemic circulation (bypasses the liver); avoids gastrointestinal enzymes and acidity.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Sublingual and Buccal administration?
flashcards.back:
Sublingual: under the tongue.
Buccal: between the cheek and gum.
Both provide rapid absorption and bypass the liver.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of the rectal (Rectal) route?
flashcards.back: Most of the drug bypasses the liver; avoids digestive enzymes; suitable for large volumes and for patients who are vomiting, unconscious, or uncooperative (e.g., children).

flashcards.front: What is the disadvantage of the rectal route?
flashcards.back: Absorption is slow, irregular, and often incomplete; may irritate the rectal mucosa.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of the inhalation route?
flashcards.back: Suitable for volatile compounds, gases, and aerosols; can be self-administered; used for local or systemic effect.

flashcards.front: What is Intrathecal injection? Where is it administered?
flashcards.back: Injection into the subarachnoid space between vertebrae L2 and L3.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Topical and Transdermal?
flashcards.back:
Topical: local effect with high local concentration and minimal systemic effect.
Transdermal: absorbed through the skin into the systemic circulation for a systemic effect (e.g., Nitroglycerin patch).

flashcards.front: What is Intra-articular injection? When is it used?
flashcards.back: Injection directly into a joint. Used, for example, to inject Corticosteroids into a joint affected by rheumatoid arthritis.

flashcards.front: What is the duration of effect of SC pellets?
flashcards.back: 3–6 months — implanted as subcutaneous granules for slow, long-term release (e.g., steroid hormones).

flashcards.front: What is the bioavailability of intravenous (IV) injection?
flashcards.back: 100% (the drug enters the circulation directly).

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of IV injection?
flashcards.back: Rapid effect; suitable for emergencies and unconscious patients; bypasses hepatic metabolism; suitable for large volumes and irritant drugs.

flashcards.front: What are the disadvantages of IV injection?
flashcards.back: Not suitable for suspensions or oily solutions; difficult to reverse after injection; requires strict asepsis; highest risk to vital functions.

flashcards.front: What is the advantage of subcutaneous (SC) injection?
flashcards.back: Longer duration of action (slower release); suitable for suspensions and oils; but onset is slow and it is not suitable for irritants or large volumes (~1 ml).

flashcards.front: What is the maximum volume for IM injection?
flashcards.back: Approximately 2–5 ml; suitable for suspensions, oils, and irritant drugs (avoid in patients on anticoagulants — risk of hematoma).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between enteral and parenteral administration?
flashcards.back: Enteral: via the gastrointestinal tract (oral/rectal); Parenteral: bypasses it (IV/IM/SC).

flashcards.front: What is the advantage of the sublingual route?
flashcards.back: Rapid absorption and bypass of first-pass hepatic metabolism.

flashcards.front: What is First-pass metabolism?
flashcards.back: Metabolism of a drug in the liver after absorption from the intestine and before reaching the systemic circulation → reduces bioavailability of oral drugs.

mcqs.q: Which route of drug administration provides 100% bioavailability?
mcqs.explanation: IV is the only route that delivers the drug directly into the circulation without losses, so Bioavailability = 100%.

mcqs.q: A drug given sublingually avoids which pharmacological process?
mcqs.explanation: The sublingual route drains directly into the systemic circulation, bypassing the portal circulation and the liver, thus avoiding first-pass metabolism.

mcqs.q: Propranolol requires an oral dose 20-40 times larger than its IV dose because of:
mcqs.explanation: Propranolol undergoes extensive first-pass metabolism in the liver before reaching the systemic circulation, requiring much higher oral doses.

mcqs.q: Which route is CONTRAINDICATED in patients on anticoagulant therapy due to risk of hematoma?
mcqs.explanation: IM is contraindicated in patients on anticoagulants due to the risk of hematoma formation within the muscle.

mcqs.q: The Mantoux test for TB screening uses which route?
mcqs.explanation: The Mantoux test uses intradermal injection to test the immune reaction against Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

mcqs.q: What is the maximum injection volume for subcutaneous (SC) administration?
mcqs.explanation: The maximum volume for SC injection is approximately 1 ml. For IM it is approximately 2–5 ml.

mcqs.q: Rectal administration is preferred in all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Patients requiring rapid drug action for emergencies
mcqs.explanation: Rectal absorption is slow and irregular, making it unsuitable for emergencies requiring rapid effect. IV is ideal for emergencies.

mcqs.q: Which drug is given transdermally to facilitate smoking cessation?
mcqs.explanation: The nicotine transdermal patch is used to assist in smoking cessation.

mcqs.q: Inhalation route is suitable for all of the following EXCEPT:
mcqs.explanation: Inhalation is suitable for volatile compounds, gases, and aerosols. Large-volume oily solutions are not suitable for inhalation.

mcqs.q: Intrathecal injection is administered between which vertebrae?
mcqs.explanation: Intrathecal injection is administered into the subarachnoid space between vertebrae L2 and L3.
IV delivers the drug completely into the circulation directly.
Sublingual absorption drains directly into the systemic vein, bypassing the liver.
